skill.name: flow-nexus-swarm
skill.description: Deploy and coordinate Flow Nexus cloud swarms, agents, workflows, queues, templates, and monitoring from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.summary: Orchestrate Flow Nexus cloud swarms and event-driven workflows for AI agent teams.
skill.author: ruvnet

security_audit.summary: AI review found no confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection in SKILL.md. Most static findings are false positives from Markdown code fences or natural language, but the skill does require trusted external commands, npm/npx execution, and a cloud Flow Nexus service.

medium_findings.title: External package execution and MCP server setup
medium_findings.description: The setup guidance asks users to install flow-nexus globally, run npx commands, and add an MCP server through Claude Code. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it executes third-party package code and grants a cloud MCP service operational access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit setup instructions and directly execute npm/npx package code. The intent appears legitimate, but users must trust the package and service account.

medium_findings.title: Cloud orchestration can expose task data to an external service
medium_findings.description: The skill centers on Flow Nexus cloud orchestration and links to the Flow Nexus platform and repositories. Users may send project descriptions, workflow inputs, agent tasks, and execution metadata to the service during normal use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation repeatedly describes cloud-based execution and external platform resources. I did not find hidden exfiltration code, so the concern is data exposure through intended cloud use.

medium_findings.title: Claude Flow hook commands execute local tooling
medium_findings.description: The integration section recommends npx claude-flow hook commands. These commands are expected for Claude Flow integration, but they still execute local package tooling and may process task metadata.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and framed as integration steps, not hidden behavior. The risk is moderate because npx execution and task metadata processing depend on trusted local tooling.

low_findings.title: Markdown fences misclassified as Ruby shell backticks
low_findings.description: The many external_commands alerts point to Markdown code fences around JavaScript and Bash examples. Most are documentation examples for MCP tool calls, not Ruby backtick shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown fenced examples and contain no Ruby backtick syntax. Only the Bash setup sections are actionable external command guidance.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are natural-language false positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts match ordinary documentation terms such as description, designs, destroy, and workflow text. I found no hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or cryptographic API usage in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged lines shows product documentation and swarm terminology, not cryptographic implementation. No evidence found for weak algorithm use.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documentation and support links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL alerts point to the Flow Nexus platform, documentation, and issues pages. These are visible support resources, not covert network destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in a Support and Resources section and are clearly disclosed. No evidence found that the skill automatically sends data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance alert is a hook command false positive
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert maps to a post-task Claude Flow hook command. It exports task metrics for integration and does not inspect host identity, processes, users, or network configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a documented post-task hook invocation with a task id. I found no reconnaissance commands such as uname, whoami, ps, ifconfig, or environment dumping.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-party command execution in setup instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes npm, npx, and Claude MCP setup commands. Users should verify the package source and understand the MCP server permissions before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These are direct external command instructions. They are not hidden, but they create a supply-chain and permission risk common to MCP setup flows.

dangerous_patterns.title: External cloud platform dependency
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requires an active Flow Nexus account and cloud platform access. Sensitive project data should be reviewed before being submitted to remote workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The requirement and platform URLs are explicit in the documentation. The risk is based on intended remote service use, not covert behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable ways to coordinate many AI agents across development, research, and operations work. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured Flow Nexus patterns for swarms, workflows, queues, templates, and monitoring.
actual_capabilities: Initializes Flow Nexus swarms with hierarchical, mesh, ring, or star topologies.
Spawns researcher, coder, analyst, optimizer, and coordinator agents with named capabilities.
Creates event-driven workflows with dependencies, triggers, retries, priorities, and async execution.
Monitors swarm status, workflow status, queues, audit trails, and execution streams.
Applies reusable templates for development, research, CI/CD, data, security, and enterprise workflows.
Documents Flow Nexus CLI and MCP setup for Claude Code integration.
limitations: Requires a Flow Nexus MCP server and an active Flow Nexus account.
Cloud workflows may send task inputs and execution metadata to the Flow Nexus service.
Examples depend on the installed Flow Nexus package and available account permissions.
The skill is orchestration guidance and does not include standalone local agent runtime code.

use_cases.title: Coordinate product development work
use_cases.description: Use specialized agents for requirements, backend, frontend, database, integration, and QA steps in one tracked workflow.

use_cases.title: Run parallel research projects
use_cases.description: Create a mesh swarm that gathers sources, analyzes findings, and produces a coordinated research summary.

use_cases.title: Automate DevOps pipelines
use_cases.description: Model CI/CD, security scanning, staging deployment, smoke tests, production deployment, and rollback logic.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic swarm
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Flow Nexus swarm for a small coding task. Recommend the topology, agent count, agent roles, and first task to orchestrate.

prompt_templates.title: Build a workflow plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Flow Nexus workflow for my project. Include steps, dependencies, triggers, retry settings, and monitoring checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Select a reusable template
prompt_templates.scenario: Use templates to speed up implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Flow Nexus templates for this goal and recommend the best one. Explain overrides for agent count, strategy, and workflow priority.

prompt_templates.title: Orchestrate a multi-phase program
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a multi-swarm Flow Nexus program with research, development, testing, and deployment phases. Include handoffs, metrics, cleanup, and risk controls.

output_examples.input: Create a swarm for a full-stack web app.
output_examples.output: A hierarchical swarm plan with a coordinator, backend coder, frontend coder, database specialist, and QA analyst. The plan includes dependent workflow steps from requirements through testing.
A monitoring checklist covering queue status, workflow status, execution metrics, audit trail review, retry state, and cleanup actions after completion.

output_examples.input: Choose a template for a code review workflow.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to start from the code-review template, reduce agent count for a small repository, and add security and performance review checkpoints.

best_practices: Choose the simplest topology that matches the task dependency structure.
Use async execution and queue monitoring for long-running or high-volume workflows.
Destroy unused swarms and review audit trails after each workflow run.
anti_patterns: Do not send secrets, customer data, or private source details without approval for cloud processing.
Do not run npm or npx setup commands without verifying the package source and version.
Do not create large swarms before defining clear roles, dependencies, and cleanup steps.

faq.question: Does this skill run Flow Nexus by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides orchestration guidance and MCP call patterns. You still need the Flow Nexus MCP server and account access.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes Claude Code MCP setup guidance and is marked compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What swarm topologies does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers hierarchical, mesh, ring, and star topologies with guidance on when each pattern fits.

faq.question: Does it support workflow automation?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes workflows with steps, dependencies, triggers, retries, priorities, audit trails, and queue monitoring.

faq.question: What security review is needed before use?
faq.answer: Review the Flow Nexus package, MCP permissions, account access, and any data sent to cloud workflows.

faq.question: Can it help with reusable templates?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows template categories for quickstart, specialized, enterprise, and custom swarm configurations.
